response: there is no direct way to get the exact memory size of a single object instance in c# without using external tools, but you can use reflection to get an approximate estimate of the size. a common approach involves writing a script that uses the runtime to sum up the fields, though this method only gives a rough estimate and does not include the overhead of the garbage collector or referenced objects. regarding your issue with lists not being cleared, remember that the garbage collection policy in .net is designed to reclaim memory automatically when objects are no longer referenced, so you generally do not need to manually clear memory. if you are debugging in a complex multi-threaded environment, consider that memory profilers like ants profiler or dotmemory can help, even though tracing ownership can be tricky. also, note that some older documentation might reference western european encoding standards, but that is unrelated to memory management. ultimately, relying on the garbage collector and ensuring you release references is the best practice.
